coredata.dc:title: Novel fluorescence probe based on bright emitted carbon dots for ClO− detection in real water samples and living cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jiaqing
Ye, Shuai
Li, Hao
Song, Jun
Qu, Junle
coredata.dc:description: Low-toxic and environmentally friendly carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively applied in various fields. CDs usually demonstrate excellent selectivity and high sensitivity, especially in ion detection. However, the most commonly used CDs are excited by ultraviolet (UV) light and emit weak fluorescence light, limiting their application in some fields. Herein, novel fluorine and nitrogen codoped carbon dots (FNCDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method and used as a fluorescent probe for ion detection. The FNCDs were excited by blue light and emitted strong green fluorescence, and the photoluminescence quantum yield was as high as 56.7%. The fluorescence of the FNCDs could be rapidly quenched by ClO− ions, indicating their potential application for ClO− detection. The fluorescence of the FNCDs was quenched by ClO− ions in less than 1 min, and the intensity of the fluorescence decreased linearly as the ClO− concentration increased from 0 to 20 μM. The detection limit was calculated to be as low as 8.2 nM, indicating high sensitivity of the FNCDs probe. The quench effect of the ClO− ions on the FNCDs probe fluorescence was not affected by other ions, demonstrating excellent selectivity of the FNCDs probe. Because of their excellent biological compatibility, the FNCDs were also successfully used to identify exogenous ClO− in living cells. These FNCDs have promising prospects as novel sensitive and inexpensive probes for the detection of pollutants and in the pathological studies of clinical diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FNCDs
High quantum yield
ClO− detection
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity assay and cellular imaging of FNCDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61975132 , 61775145 , 61835009 , and 81727804 ), National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2018YFC0910600 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shenzhen University (No. 2019108 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2019M650211 ), and the Postgraduate Innovation Development Fund Project of Shenzhen .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue curve), FL excitation spectrum (black curve), and emission spectrum (red curve) of the FNCDs; the inset is the photographs of the real samples under daylight (left) and under a 365 nm UV lamp (right); (b) FL emission spectra of the FNCDs with different excitation wavelengths; (c) fluorescence decay traces of the FNCDs; (d) PLQY of the FNCDs; (e) typical TEM image of the FNCDs; the inset of (e) is the corresponding size distribution; (f) FT-IR spectrum of the FNCDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of the FNCDs; (b), (c), (d), and (e): corresponding high-resolution C1s, N1s, O1s, and F1s spectra, respectively.

Normalized FL intensity of FNCDs (a) in NaCl aqueous solution with different concentrations; (b) at different temperatures; (c) under UV light irradiation for different times; and (d) at different pH values without or with ClO−.

(a) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of FNCDs quenched by ClO− (20 μM) ions and contradistinctive ions (2 mM); (b) effect of contradistinctive ions (2 mM) on the fluorescence intensity of ClO−-quenched FNCDs; and (c) photographs of the FNCDs colloidal solution quenched by different ions under excitation by a 365 nm UV lamp.
(a) Relationship between the ClO− concentration and the fluorescence intensity of the FNCDs; (b) fluorescence quench ratio of the FNCDs with added ClO− concentrations.

(a) Effect of FNCD concentrations on 4T1 cell viability (incubation duration: 24 h); (b), (c), and (d) confocal images of living 4T1 cells incubated with 20 μg mL−1 FNCDs solution for 30 min; (e), (f), and (g) confocal images of living 4T1 cells incubated with 20 μg mL−1 FNCDs solution mixed with 20 μM ClO− for 30 min.
Comparison of ClO− detection using different probes.
ClO− detection in real water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Novel fluorescence probe based on bright emitted carbon dots for ClO− detection in real water samples and living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Biomedical Optics and Photonics (CBOP) & College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Biomedical Optics and Photonics (CBOP) & College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering
Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Biomedical Optics and Photonics (CBOP) & College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low-toxic and environmentally friendly carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively applied in various fields. CDs usually demonstrate excellent selectivity and high sensitivity, especially in ion detection. However, the most commonly used CDs are excited by ultraviolet (UV) light and emit weak fluorescence light, limiting their application in some fields. Herein, novel fluorine and nitrogen codoped carbon dots (FNCDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method and used as a fluorescent probe for ion detection. The FNCDs were excited by blue light and emitted strong green fluorescence, and the photoluminescence quantum yield was as high as 56.7%. The fluorescence of the FNCDs could be rapidly quenched by ClO− ions, indicating their potential application for ClO− detection. The fluorescence of the FNCDs was quenched by ClO− ions in less than 1 min, and the intensity of the fluorescence decreased linearly as the ClO− concentration increased from 0 to 20 μM. The detection limit was calculated to be as low as 8.2 nM, indicating high sensitivity of the FNCDs probe. The quench effect of the ClO− ions on the FNCDs probe fluorescence was not affected by other ions, demonstrating excellent selectivity of the FNCDs probe. Because of their excellent biological compatibility, the FNCDs were also successfully used to identify exogenous ClO− in living cells. These FNCDs have promising prospects as novel sensitive and inexpensive probes for the detection of pollutants and in the pathological studies of clinical diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been the subject of widespread research because they are low-cost, low-toxic, and environmentally friendly [1–4]. CDs have excellent chemical and optical performance, such as strong photoluminescence, chemical stability, biocompatibility, and anti-photobleaching, giving them potential application in the fields of bioimaging [5–7], biosensing [8–12], ion detection [13–15], optoelectronic devices [16,17], and others [18].

Ion detection has been receiving more and more attention in the past several years as an important application of CDs. Generally, an excellent probe for ion detection must meet the following requirements: short response time, wide detection range, low detection limit, excellent selectivity, and high sensitivity. Unfortunately, although many kinds of CDs have been explored and used as fluorescence probes for ion detection, they are currently still far from ideal. The CDs synthesized by Zhao et al. had the advantages of excellent selectivity and high sensitivity for detecting [Ag+], but their fluorescence intensity was not stable at different pH values, resulting in inaccurate [Ag+] detection under different pH conditions [19]. Wang et al. used Osmanthus fragrans flowers to synthesize CDs. These CDs had a wide detection range (from 0 to 500 μM) for Fe3+, but their selectivity was very limited. Fe3+ ions were not always detected when interfering ions existed in the sample [20]. Another limitation of current CDs is that most of them that are used as probes for ion detection are excited by ultraviolet (UV) light, which limits their application in living cells or tissues because the cells or tissues could autofluoresce under UV light excitation and perturb the detection results. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the CD probe is another factor to be considered when used in living cells because CDs with a lower PLQY will only be excited by a laser with higher power, which is very harmful not only to cells but also to the CD probe. Wang et al. reported blue fluorescent CDs with a low detection limit (2.8 nM) for Hg2+ ions. However, the short excitation wavelength and low fluorescence quantum yield (18.8%) limits their further application in cells [21]. It is therefore necessary to develop a novel CD that meets all of the requirements for ion detection, including a longer excitation wavelength and high PLQY for ion detection in living cells or tissues [22].

The properties of CDs are determined by their surface state as well as local chemical and electronic properties, which are easily regulated by incorporating heteroatoms such as boron, sulfur, phosphorous, nitrogen, or fluorine into the carbon matrix. In this work, fluorine and nitrogen codoped carbon dots (FNCDs) with a green fluorescence emission and high PLQY (56.7%) were synthesized using 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid and glycine as sources via a simple hydrothermal process at relatively low temperature. The obtained FNCDs could be used as probes for ClO− detection based on a fluorescence quenching mechanism, and presented excellent selectivity and sensitivity. These FNCDs could also be used to identify exogenous ClO− in living cells owing to their excellent biocompatibility.

2,4-Difluorobenzoic acid and glycine were bought from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Metal salts (NaClO, CH3COONa, KCl, MnCl2·4H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, CdCO3, BaCl2·2H2O, Pb(NO3)2, (CH3COO)2Zn, FeCl3·6H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CuSO4, NaOH, Na2HPO4, NaSO3, KH2PO4, Na2H2PO2, NaHCO3, K2CO3, Pb(NO3)2), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were supplied by Macklin (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were used without further purification.

The morphology of the FNCDs was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope, USA). The composition of the FNCDs was analyzed by a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi multifunctional imaging electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) with a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corp., USA) was used to identify the chemical structures. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the FNCDs were recorded by using a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and Fluorolog-3 steady-state spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Scientific), respectively. The fluorescence (FL) lifetime and quantum yield (QY) were measured on an FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer.

The FNCDs were prepared using glycine and 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid via a cost-effective one-pot hydrothermal method. First, 1 g glycine and 1.5 g 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid were dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water. Then, the dissolved solution was added into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After allowing to cool to ambient temperature, the obtained brownish-yellow solution was dialyzed against deionized water in a 500 Da dialysis tube for 3 days, changing the ultrapure water every 12 h to remove impurities. Finally, the dialyzed product was filtered by a polyethersulfone (PES) membrane having a pore size of 0.22 μm and then lyophilized to obtain the FNCDs powder.

A mixture of the FNCDs (40 μg mL−1) and an aqueous solution containing the ion under investigation (2 mM) was subjected to fluorescence measurements. The fluorescence emission spectra of the quenched samples were measured after excitation by a 466 nm laser. All quenching experiments were conducted in triplicate. Photographs of the FNCDs colloidal solutions were taken with a digital camera under excitation by a 365 nm UV lamp.

Real samples from tap water or swimming pools were first filtered through a PES membrane to remove impurities, followed by spiking with different concentrations of ClO− to obtain the target solutions (tap water: 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 μM; swimming pool water: 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 μM). The FNCDs were then added into each of these samples, and the ClO− concentration in the solutions was calculated according to the measured fluorescence intensity. All quenching experiments and measurements were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay and cellular imaging of FNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse breast cancer cells (4T1 cells) were cultured in a standard medium at 37 °C for 24 h under an air atmosphere with 5% CO2. The cells were first incubated for 24 h in a 96-well plate, then a series of diluted FNCDs colloidal solutions with concentrations from 0 to 400 μg mL−1 were added. The cell viability of all samples was determined using an MTT assay after 48 h incubation. All measurements were performed three times.

The 4T1 cells were incubated with 20 μL of FNCDs colloidal solution (5 mg mL−1) for 30 min at 37 °C under an air atmosphere with 5% CO2. After washing with PBS three times, the 4T1 cells were imaged by a Nikon A1R MP+ multifunction confocal fluorescence microscope (λex = 488 nm; detection scale: 500–550 nm).

The absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured to investigate the optical properties of the FNCDs. The absorption spectrum of the FNCDs in an aqueous solution is shown in Fig. 1a; the absorption peaks at 236 and 283 nm correspond to π → π* (CC bond) and n → π* (CO and CC bond) [23,24] transitions. Another absorption peak at 466 nm is attributed to the excitonic absorption band. The excitation peak of the prepared FNCDs was centered at 466 nm (Fig. 1a) and the emission peak was centered at 508 nm with a long tail in the long-wavelength direction (Fig. 1a). Therefore, the FNCDs emitted bright green luminescent light under irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 1a, inset). The fluorescence spectra of the FNCDs excited by different wavelengths are shown in Fig. 1b. It was found the emission peaks did not shift as the excitation wavelength changed from 300 to 480 nm, indicating band-gap luminescence. The band-gap luminescence was mainly attributed to the incorporation of the element fluorine in the CDs [25,26]. The average lifetime of the FNCDs was calculated as approximately 4.89 ns by multi-exponential decay traces (Fig. 1c). The PLQY of the FNCDs was estimated to be 56.7% by the direct method, as shown in Fig. 1d. The TEM image (Fig. 1e) indicated that the morphology of the monodispersed FNCDs was very uniform, and the size ranged from 3.5 to 6.5 nm. The FT-IR spectrum of the FNCDs is shown in Fig. 1f. The characteristic peaks at 3440, 3120, 1705, and 1510 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of –OH, the flexural vibrations of N-CH, the asymmetric stretching vibrations of CO, and the flexural vibrations of C-C=N, respectively [27]. In addition, the two absorption peaks at 990 and 1260 cm−1 correspond to the aromatic CF stretching vibrations.

The chemical composition of the FNCDs was analyzed by XPS. As shown Fig. 2a, four peaks were found at 284.4, 399.6, 532.4, and 687.6 eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s, and F1s, respectively, and their contents were 61.3%, 14.3%, 19.2%, and 5.2%, respectively, indicating the incorporation of nitrogen and fluorine into the carbon matrix. High-resolution XPS was further conducted to analyze the chemical bonds. The C1s spectrum was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.88, 286.13, 286.76, and 288.13 eV (Fig. 2b), corresponding to four kinds of carbon bonds, including C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N, CO, and CF. The N1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.51, 400.26 and 401.51 eV, indicating the existence of three nitrogen bonds, including C–N=C, NC, and NH (Fig. 2c). The O1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks, corresponding to two types of oxygen bonds: CO (531.51 eV) and CO (533.01 eV) (Fig. 2d). Deconvolution of the F1s spectrum indicated the presence of two types of fluorine bonds: semi-ionic CF (686.51 eV) and covalent CF (687.26 eV) (Fig. 2e) [28–30].

The stability of the FNCDs under different conditions was investigated. Fig. 3a shows the stability of the FNCDs in aqueous NaCl solutions with concentrations of NaCl from 0 to 1.0 mol L−1. Although the fluorescence intensity of the FNCDs was slightly affected by the NaCl concentration, there was excellent stability of the FNCDs in a NaCl solution with a high concentration of 1.0 mol L−1. The effect of temperature on the FNCDs was also investigated from 30 to 100 °C (boiling point of water), as shown in Fig. 3b. Compared with that at ambient temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the FNCDs at 100 °C was reduced only 15%, indicating excellent temperature stability of the FNCDs and their potential application at high temperature. Photostability was also a key factor to be determined. Fig. 3c shows the effect of exposure time on the fluorescence intensity of the FNCDs. It was found that the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged after the FNCDs were exposed to UV light (365 nm) for 1 h, demonstrating excellent photobleaching resistance. Fig. 3d shows the influence of solution pH on FNCD stability. Both the FNCDs and the ClO−-quenched FNCDs retained their original fluorescence intensity under different pH conditions, indicating the FNCDs were stable in both acid and alkaline solutions. Therefore, these FNCDs have excellent stability under different conditions and could be applied for ClO− detection in different environments.

A series of control experiments were performed to evaluate the selectivity and sensitivity of the FNCDs when used as detection probe. First, various biologically and environmentally related ions, including K+, Na+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, ClO3 −, SO4 2−, CH3COO−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, HCO3 −, SO3 2−, HPO4 2−, Cl−, F−, H2PO4 −, and H2PO2 − (2 mM) were added to the FNCDs solution. Unlike the significant fluorescence that was quenched rapidly when ClO− was added to the FNCDs colloidal solution, the quenching effect was almost negligible for FNCDs colloidal solutions that incorporated the above-mentioned ions (Fig. 4a), indicating excellent selectivity of the FNCDs probe. Images of FNCD samples mixed with various metal ions under excitation of 365 nm UV light show strong fluorescence (Fig. 4c). The fluorescence intensity of the FNCDs colloidal solution was quenched completely when the concentration of added ClO− ions was 20 μM, indicating the sensitivity of the FNCDs probe. In order to confirm the ability of the FNCDs probe to detect ClO− in the presence of potential interference, the photoluminescence spectra of ClO−-quenched FNCDs colloidal solutions mixed with other ions were recorded (Fig. 4b). No influence of the added ions on the fluorescence intensity of the quenched FNCDs colloidal solution was observed, further indicating the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the FNCDs.

Fig. 5a shows the influence of ClO− concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the FNCDs. The fluorescence intensity of the FNCDs colloidal solutions were gradually weakened with increased ClO− concentration and was quenched completely when the ClO− concentration was as high as 20 μM. Meanwhile, it was found that the emission peak of the FNCDs red shifted slightly with increased ClO− concentration. The red-shifted emission of the FNCDs may be related to the added ClO− ions. When the fluorescence of the FNCDs was quenched by ClO− ions, the surface composition of the FNCDs was changed because of the incorporation of Cl or O elements, which led to the red shift of the fluorescence of the FNCDs. Fig. 5b presents the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the ClO− concentration, which had a good linear correlation from 0 to 20 μM with R2 = 0.9983. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be as low as 8.2 nM (three times the standard deviation) [31]. The LOD value of the FNCDs probe for ClO− detection was much lower than that of the CDs probes previously reported [32–39], as shown in Table 1 .

The high sensitivity and selectivity of the FNCDs as a ClO− probe suggested the feasibility of FNCDs as a probe for detecting ClO− in practical, complex samples. Therefore, the FNCDs probe was used to detect ClO− ions in real water samples. We obtained two tap water samples, one from the lab (Tap water 1) and one from the kitchen (Tap water 2); and two swimming pool water samples, one from an outdoor swimming pool (Swimming pool water 1) and one from an indoor swimming pool (Swimming pool water 2). As shown in Table 2 , ClO− solutions with a concentration of 0.10, 0.50, or 1.00 μM were added into the tap water samples. The recovery rates were calculated to be 98.2–101.1%, 102.4–103.5%, and 97.7–102.2%, respectively, and the relative standard deviations were 4.23–6.78%, 2.12–4.12%, and 3.14–4.45%, respectively. For the swimming pool water samples, ClO− solutions with concentrations of 1.00, 2.00, and 3.00 μM were added. The recovery rates were calculated to be 103.0–105.0%, 103.3–107.3%, and 98.4–104.7%, respectively, and the relative standard deviations were calculated to be 3.47–5.12%, 3.12–3.47%, and 4.23–4.78%, respectively. Thus, this method exhibits great recovery rate and accuracy, and shows potential application for the quantifiable detection of ClO− ions in practical water samples.

The FNCDs were also used to detect ClO− ions in living cells. First, the cytotoxicity of the FNCDs was evaluated via the MTT method. The 4T1 cells were incubated in culture solutions mixed with different concentrations of FNCDs for 48 h. As shown in Fig. 6a, it was observed that cell viability reached 80% even when the FNCD concentration reached 400.0 μg mL−1, indicating the ultralow toxicity of the FNCDs toward living cells. The FNCDs were then used to detect intracellular ClO− ions. When the living cells were incubated in a culture solution mixed with 20 μg mL−1 FNCDs for 30 min, green fluorescence was clearly observed in the cells and most of the FNCDs were found to have accumulated in the nuclei or cytosol (Fig. 6b–d). However, when the culture solution was mixed with 20 μM ClO− ions, the green fluorescence of the cells gradually disappeared after 30 min of incubation, as shown in Fig. 6e–g, indicating the added ClO− diffused into the cells and the fluorescence of the labelled FNCDs probe was quenched. Therefore, the FNCDs probe was a suitable candidate for ClO− detection in living cells.

FNCDs with bright green emission and high PLQY (56.7%) were prepared via a facile one-pot hydrothermal process using glycine and 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid. It was found that the novel CDs could be used as a probe for ClO− detection with high selectivity and sensitivity. The fluorescence intensity of the FNCDs was very rapidly quenched by ClO− ions and without interference from other ions. The detection range of ClO− by the FNCDs probes was very wide, from 0 to 20 μM, and the detection limit was an extremely low 8.2 nM. Furthermore, the FNCDs were incubated in living 4T1 cells and used to identify exogenous ClO− ions with the assistance of cell imaging.

Jiaqing Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Investigation. Shuai Ye: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Hao Li: Data curation. Jun Song: Validation,Supervision. Junle Qu: Resource, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61975132, 61775145, 61835009, and 81727804), National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2018YFC0910600), Natural Science Foundation of Shenzhen University (No. 2019108), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2019M650211), and the Postgraduate Innovation Development Fund Project of Shenzhen.

Nano Today

TrAC Trends Anal. Chem.

Chem. Mater.

Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. K. Sonkar, M. B. Roy, G. Dipak. Water oluble carbon nano-onions from wood wool as growth promoters for gram plants. Nanoscale, 2012, 4, 7670-7675.

Adv. Mater.

Nanoscale Adv.

Green Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. M. Liu, B. B. Chen, C. M. Li. Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications. Green chemistry, 2019, 21, 3, 449-471.

Coord. Chem. Rev.
